example_sentence: La ofrenda tiene flores de cempasúchil y fotos de los difuntos.
example_translation: The altar has marigold flowers and photos of the deceased.

translation: the marigold flower (used in Día de Muertos)
example_sentence: El aroma del cempasúchil guía a los difuntos de regreso.
example_translation: The scent of marigolds guides the deceased back.

example_sentence: Don Miguel siempre nos da buenos consejos.
example_translation: Don Miguel always gives us good advice.

example_sentence: La quinceañera de mi prima fue más grande que una boda.
example_translation: My cousin's quinceañera was bigger than a wedding.

example_sentence: Fuimos al zócalo a escuchar el grito del presidente.
example_translation: We went to the town square to hear the president's cry of independence.

example_sentence: En las posadas siempre hay piñata y ponche.
example_translation: At posadas there's always a piñata and ponche.

example_sentence: Los tacos, las garnachas y el elote son antojitos típicos.
example_translation: Tacos, garnachas, and corn on the cob are typical street snacks.

example_sentence: Al despedirse, mi abuela siempre dice: ¡Que Dios te bendiga!
example_translation: When saying goodbye, my grandmother always says: God bless you!

translation: in between / caught between two worlds (Nahuatl concept)
example_sentence: La cultura mexicana vive en nepantla entre lo indígena y lo español.
example_translation: Mexican culture lives in nepantla between indigenous and Spanish heritage.

example_sentence: En las Fiestas Patrias siempre comemos pozole rojo.
example_translation: During Independence celebrations we always eat red pozole.

translation: come on, sit down! (warm hospitality invitation)
example_sentence: ¡Ándale, siéntate que hay de todo y ya está listo!
example_translation: Come on, sit down — there's plenty and it's ready!
